items.q: What is the difference between a wood stain and a varnish?
items.correct: Stain colours the wood; varnish provides a protective surface coat.
items.random_wrong: Stain is permanent, varnish always peels off.
items.why: Students confuse colouring (stain penetrates) with protecting (varnish sits on top). Many products combine both, but their primary functions differ. Often stain is applied first, then varnish.

items.q: What property do shape memory alloys have?
items.correct: They return to a pre-set shape when heated.
items.misconception: They remember every shape they have ever been bent into.
items.why: Students think SMAs are infinitely programmable. They actually have ONE 'remembered' shape, set during high-temperature treatment. Heating restores that shape; the alloy doesn't store a history of deformations.

items.q: What is FEA used for in product design?
items.correct: Simulating how a product will respond to forces, heat, or vibration before manufacture.
items.misconception: Final exam analysis of student designs.
items.random_wrong: Counting the elements in a chemical compound.
items.why: Students unfamiliar with the acronym guess at it. FEA = Finite Element Analysis: dividing a part into many small elements and simulating physical behaviour computationally - saves prototyping costs.

items.q: Whose 1955 Designing for People standardised anthropometric data?
items.why: Correct answer: Dreyfuss. “Loewy” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Fitts' Law. “Hick-Hyman Law” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which percentile range do designs typically accommodate?
items.why: The real figure is 95 percentile. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
